First, I'll recall how a typical tape measure works. Most tape measures have a locking mechanism to hold the tape in place when taking measurements. The lock is usually a small button or tab near the end of the tape case. When you extend the tape, you can engage the lock so the tape doesn't retract accidentally.

Now, the options are about whether to pull the locking tab up or press it down. I think about the design of the locking tab. Usually, the locking mechanism is a small lever or button on the side. When you extend the tape, you might push the tab down to lock it, which would engage the lock and prevent the tape from retracting. Alternatively, some designs might have a tab that you lift up to lock it. Wait, I need to visualize this. If the tab is on the front, pressing down might be the action. Maybe I should think about the direction. If the locking tab is up, maybe it's in the unlocked position. So pressing it down would be locking. Let me check my own experience. question_text: how to lock a tape measure.

choices.A: after extending the tape to the desired length, pull the front locking tab up until it locks into place.
choices.B: after extending the tape to the desired length, press the front locking tab down until it locks into place.
